regulator: FSA (Financial Services Agency) / JSTOA (Japan Security Token Offering Association)
instruments: Financial Instruments and Exchange Act (金融商品取引法, Act No. 25 of 1948 / 昭和二十三年法律第二十五号), Art. 2(2) (deemed securities enumeration -- 信託の受益権 (trust beneficial interests) and 集団投資スキーム持分 (collective investment scheme interests), among others) and Art. 2(3) (電子記録移転権利, 'electronically recorded transferable rights' / ERTR) -- both confirmed by direct text fetch via the e-Gov Law API, https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/api/2/law_data/323AC0000000025; Art. 2(3)'s statutory text reads: '電子情報処理組織を用いて移転することができる財産的価値（電子機器その他の物に電子的方法により記録されるものに限る。）に表示される場合（流通性その他の事情を勘案して内閣府令で定める場合を除く。）' (Japanese-language primary text, quoted verbatim, not translated here)

Act No. 28 of 2019 (令和元年法律第28号), '情報通信技術の進展に伴う金融取引の多様化に対応するための資金決済に関する法律等の一部を改正する法律' -- the same single amending Act cited in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar for its Payment Services Act (PSA) changes; this Act also inserted the ERTR provisions into FIEA Art. 2(2)-(3) and extended the Type I financial instruments business perimeter to cover them; took effect 1 May 2020 by Cabinet Order No. 141 of 3 April 2020 -- the same commencement date as the PSA-side custody/derivatives hardening tracked in the Exchanges pillar, because one statute amended both Acts at once

FIEA Art. 4(1), requiring a securities registration statement (有価証券届出書) filed with the Prime Minister before a public offering or secondary distribution of Art. 2(1) ('first-paragraph') securities -- confirmed by direct text fetch via the e-Gov Law API; the statutory text reads: '有価証券の募集（特定組織再編成発行手続を含む。第十三条及び第十五条第二項から第六項までを除き、以下この章及び次章において同じ。）又は有価証券の売出し...は、発行者が当該有価証券の募集又は売出しに関し内閣総理大臣に届出をしているものでなければ、することができない。' (Japanese-language primary text, quoted verbatim, not translated here) -- ERTRs, once they satisfy Art. 2(3), are treated as Art. 2(1) securities for this purpose, which is the structural mechanism giving security tokens prospectus-style disclosure

FIEA Art. 29, requiring registration as a Type I Financial Instruments Business Operator (第一種金融商品取引業者) to deal in, broker, or intermediate offerings of Art. 2(1) securities including ERTRs -- this pillar's description of Art. 29 rests on FIEA's well-established, widely secondary-corroborated Type I registration framework and on JSTOA's and FSA's own materials describing ERTR business as Type I business; the e-Gov API response for the full FIEA text was too large to return Art. 29's exact text within this session's fetch (see open_items) and Art. 29 was not independently re-quoted verbatim

FSA, current list of certified financial instruments business associations (認定金融商品取引業協会一覧), confirmed by direct fetch, https://www.fsa.go.jp/koueki/koueki10b.html -- lists 一般社団法人日本STO協会 (Japan STO Association / JSTOA) among five FIEA Art. 78(1)-certified associations, with a stated founding date of 令和元年10月１日 (1 October 2019) and a quoted statutory purpose: '正会員の電子記録移転権利等の売買その他の取引等を公正かつ円滑にならしめ、金融商品取引業の健全な発展を図り、もって投資者の保護に資することを目的とする' (Japanese-language primary text, quoted verbatim, not translated here); secondary legal-press sources place JSTOA's actual certification date at 30 April 2020, effective 1 May 2020 -- the same commencement date as the FIEA ERTR amendment itself -- but this research could not independently re-confirm that exact date via a live primary fetch this session (see open_items)

JSTOA (一般社団法人日本STO協会) self-regulatory rules (定款・自主規制規則等・諸規則), including rules on the handling of offerings of ERTRs (電子記録移転権利の募集の取扱い等に関する規則) and, since 2023, rules on trading ERTRs on proprietary trading systems (私設取引システムにおける電子記録移転権利の取引等に関する規則) -- binding on JSTOA's registered-operator members, layered on top of the statutory Type I FIB registration regime; confirmed by direct fetch of JSTOA's own site, https://jstoa.or.jp/

Japan Securities Dealers Association (JSDA) and JSTOA, joint monthly statistical publication '電子記録移転有価証券表示権利等の取扱状況等' (handling status of electronically recorded transferable rights representing securities, etc.), compiling JSDA's survey of member firms' tokenized-securities (トークン化有価証券) holdings/dealings together with JSTOA's survey of its regular members' ERTR holdings/dealings -- confirmed by direct fetch of JSDA's statistics index page, https://www.jsda.or.jp/shiryoshitsu/toukei/sttoukei/index.html, most recently updated 1 July 2026, and of JSTOA's own announcement page for the corresponding release, https://jstoa.or.jp/news/detail/225
standing_summary: Japan regulates the tokenization of real-world and financial assets by amending the existing Financial Instruments and Exchange Act (FIEA), not by enacting a dedicated security-token or tokenization statute -- the same amendment-based pattern that runs through every pillar of this jurisdiction's regime. The operative mechanism is the concept of 'electronically recorded transferable rights' (電子記録移転権利, ERTR), inserted into FIEA Art. 2(3) by Act No. 28 of 2019 (令和元年法律第28号) -- the single omnibus amending Act that, in the same commencement event, also hardened the PSA's crypto-asset custody and derivatives rules tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) and Dealing/Custody pillars -- and which took effect 1 May 2020 under Cabinet Order No. 141 of 3 April 2020. FIEA Art. 2(2) lists a set of instruments that the Act deems securities ('みなし有価証券' / 'second-paragraph securities'), including trust beneficial interests (信託の受益権) and collective investment scheme interests (集団投資スキーム持分, the category used for most real-estate and fund-style tokenization structures); these instruments are ordinarily treated as comparatively illiquid and subject to lighter disclosure. Art. 2(3), fetched and confirmed directly via the e-Gov Law API (law_id 323AC0000000025), reclassifies any such Art. 2(2) right as an ERTR -- and therefore as a first-paragraph, Art. 2(1)-equivalent security subject to full prospectus-style disclosure -- whenever it is 'displayed in a form of proprietary value that can be transferred using an electronic data processing system,' i.e. tokenized on a system enabling electronic transfer, unless a Cabinet Office Ordinance carve-out for low-circulation cases applies ('流通性その他の事情を勘案して内閣府令で定める場合を除く' in the Act's own words). The policy logic, consistent across FSA's own materials and independent commentary, is that tokenization materially increases an instrument's practical tradability, so the law responds by upgrading its disclosure and business-conduct treatment to match ordinary, freely tradable securities such as listed shares or corporate bonds. That upgrade has two concrete legal consequences, both confirmed by direct fetch of FIEA's current text: a public offering or secondary distribution of an ERTR requires the issuer to file a securities registration statement (有価証券届出書) with the Prime Minister under Art. 4(1), just as for any other first-paragraph security; and dealing in, brokering, or intermediating ERTR transactions requires registration as a Type I Financial Instruments Business Operator under Art. 29, the same registration category used for ordinary broker-dealers -- the more demanding of FIEA's two principal registration tiers. This pillar's Japanese-language market terminology distinguishes 'ERTR' proper (tokenized versions of instruments that started life as Art. 2(2) deemed securities, such as tokenized trust beneficial interests used in most real-estate structures) from 'tokenized securities' (トークン化有価証券, tokenized versions of instruments that were already Art. 2(1) securities, such as tokenized corporate bonds), while both are swept together under the umbrella statistical and self-regulatory term '電子記録移転有価証券表示権利等' -- a distinction this research corroborates only through secondary legal/industry-glossary sources rather than a single FSA or JSTOA primary page stating it explicitly (see open_items), but which matters because it explains why Japan's digital-bond issuances and its real-estate security token offerings sit under the same regulatory perimeter despite starting from different points in FIEA's securities taxonomy. Self-regulation of this pillar sits with the Japan STO Association (JSTOA, 一般社団法人日本STO協会): FSA's own current, live-fetched list of certified financial instruments business associations confirms JSTOA holds certification under FIEA Art. 78(1), records its founding date as 1 October 2019, and quotes its statutory purpose verbatim as ensuring fair and smooth trading of ERTRs by its regular members; JSTOA's own site confirms it maintains binding self-regulatory rules on the handling of ERTR offerings and, since 2023, on ERTR trading through proprietary trading systems. Japan does have a real, functioning issued market under this framework -- digital corporate bonds and real-estate-backed security token offerings are issued and traded under FIEA-registered Type I business operators on a recurring basis -- which this pillar evidences through JSDA and JSTOA's own joint official monthly statistical publication on ERTR/tokenized-securities handling (most recently updated 1 July 2026), rather than through naming any individual issuer, arranger, or platform, consistent with this project's named-entity rule: the site describes the regime, not the firms operating within it. This research confirmed the statistics program exists and is current, but could not machine-extract the underlying Excel file's exact cumulative issuance figures this session, so no specific yen total is asserted as fact here.

key_links.label: e-Gov Law API: Financial Instruments and Exchange Act (金融商品取引法, Act No. 25 of 1948) -- full text, JSON
e-Gov: Financial Instruments and Exchange Act, consolidated text (interactive page)

Japanese Law Translation (Ministry of Justice): Financial Instruments and Exchange Act, English courtesy translation -- NOTE: stale, last reflects amendments through Act No. 32 of 2010, predates the 2019 ERTR amendment entirely
FSA: list of certified financial instruments business associations (認定金融商品取引業協会一覧), confirming JSTOA's FIEA Art. 78(1) certification
JSTOA: investor explainer on security tokens / ERTRs
JSDA: statistics on the handling status of electronically recorded transferable rights representing securities, etc. (電子記録移転有価証券表示権利等の取扱状況等)
JSTOA: announcement of October 2025 handling-status statistics (joint JSDA/JSTOA release)
FSA: bills submitted to the Diet (国会提出法案等) -- lists the pending FIEA/PSA migration bill, submitted 10 April 2026
House of Councillors (参議院): bill status page for the pending FIEA/PSA migration bill (221st Diet session, bill no. 57)

open_items: FIEA Art. 29's exact statutory text (the Type I Financial Instruments Business registration requirement that applies to dealing in, brokering, or intermediating ERTRs) was not independently re-fetched verbatim via the e-Gov Law API this session -- the API's full-text response for the whole Act was too large for the article to be returned within this session's fetch. This pillar's description of Art. 29 rests on FIEA's well-established Type I registration framework, on JSTOA's and FSA's own certification materials describing ERTR business as Type I business, and on secondary legal-commentary corroboration, not on a direct primary-text quotation obtained this session; it should be re-confirmed against the article's exact text before being treated as a verbatim citation.

JSTOA's exact FIEA certification date is reported in secondary legal-press sources as 30 April 2020 (effective 1 May 2020, the same commencement date as the ERTR amendment). FSA's own official announcement page for that recognition (searched as fsa.go.jp/news/r1/shouken/20200430.html) returned HTTP 404 on direct fetch this session. This card instead relies on FSA's own current, live-fetched list of certified associations (koueki10b.html), which confirms JSTOA's certification and founding date but does not itself display the certification date -- the exact 30 April 2020 date should be re-confirmed against a working FSA primary page before being treated as independently primary-verified.

The terminology boundary between 'ERTR' (tokenized ex-Art. 2(2) instruments, e.g. tokenized trust beneficial interests used in most real-estate structures) and 'tokenized securities' (トークン化有価証券, tokenized instruments that were already Art. 2(1) securities, e.g. tokenized corporate bonds) -- both swept under the umbrella term 電子記録移転有価証券表示権利等 used in JSDA/JSTOA's joint statistics -- is described in this card based on secondary legal/industry-glossary corroboration (used only to describe well-established structural terminology, not as the sole basis for any operative legal claim), since a single FSA or JSTOA primary page stating the distinction in exactly those terms was not located this session.

The JSDA/JSTOA joint monthly statistics on ERTR/tokenized-securities handling (most recently updated 1 July 2026 per JSDA's own statistics index page) confirm an active, officially monitored market exists, but this research could not machine-extract the underlying data file's exact cumulative issuance figures this session -- the file is published in Excel format rather than as machine-readable page text, the same class of extraction limitation flagged for PDF sources in this jurisdiction's Stablecoins pillar and in the Switzerland jurisdiction's Tokenization pillar. No specific yen total is stated as fact in this card for that reason; a privately compiled market-size estimate located during this research (from a named market participant, not JSDA/JSTOA official statistics) was deliberately excluded, consistent with this project's rule that primary sources, not secondary industry estimates, are the sole basis for factual claims.

Boundary with the Stablecoins pillar: trust-type stablecoins (特定信託受益権), defined in PSA Art. 2(9) and covered by the 2022 and 2025 PSA amendments (including the 2025 amendment's relaxation of their backing-asset rules), are tracked in the Stablecoins pillar, not here -- even though 特定信託受益権 and the 信託の受益権 deemed-securities category that can become an ERTR both originate in overlapping trust-beneficial-interest territory, the PSA/electronic-payment-instrument framing and the FIEA/ERTR framing are legally distinct regimes, and this pillar covers only the FIEA/ERTR side.

Whether the pending 'Bill for Partial Amendment of the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Payment Services Act' (submitted to the Diet 10 April 2026; passed the House of Representatives 11 June 2026; confirmed, via direct fetch of both FSA's own bill-tracking page and the House of Councillors' own bill-status page this session, still pending in the House of Councillors' Financial Affairs Committee as of 12 July 2026, with no recorded committee vote date) touches the ERTR/security-token framework described in this pillar was not confirmed this session. The bill's officially stated target, per FSA's own materials, is migrating crypto-asset (PSA-side) business regulation into FIEA, not amending the ERTR provisions themselves -- this pillar's 'in_force' status_seal reflects only the existing ERTR regime, which does not depend on that bill's enactment, and this open item should be re-checked if the bill's final enacted text becomes available.

This pillar does not address the prudential/accounting treatment of ERTR issuance and holding (Accounting Standards Board of Japan Practical Issues Task Force No. 43, 実務対応報告第43号) or crypto-asset custody/derivatives dealing, which are tracked in the Banking & Money and Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillars respectively, not duplicated here.
